"What time is it?" Alice asked a crimson rose. The rose turned its petals. "Here, time is measured in lost dreams. You've slept three memories and two eternities." Alice blinked. "But I had tea five minutes ago!" The rose laughed thornily. "Five minutes? My dear, that clock never existed. Look behind you." Alice turned: her shadow was drinking milk straight from the moon.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

A wigged toad read a map upside down. "This way leads to Yesterday, but be careful—the turns are in Tomorrow." Alice spun three times. "And if I want to go home?" The toad lowered his spectacles. "Home is a word that changes meaning each time you say it. Here, take this tablecloth—it leads to where you haven't left yet.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

Sitting on a wobbly four-legged chair, Alice watched a handless clockmaker carve seconds from dead wood. "How much for a minute?" she asked. "A butterfly's tear," he replied, "or three mouse sneezes. But I don't sell them to girls who know the time." Alice began crying needles. "Perfect," said the clockmaker, "now we can make an afternoon.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

"Why are the playing cards watering the roses?" whispered Alice. The Queen of Hearts stormed past, red with fury. "Because white lies, my child. Only red tells the time: four-fifteen of rage." A white rose began to cough. "Don't listen—she confuses seasons with moods." Alice took one red and one white rose. Both began humming a lullaby in unison.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Mad Hatter poured tea into a bottomless cup. "You see, Time and I fell out. Now he sneaks in through the window." Alice emptied her cup: it was full of silence. "But I'm thirsty!" "Then drink the absence of drinking," he said, handing her a spoon. "It's far more refreshing." The spoon melted like a smile. Alice drank her own bewilderment.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

A sleeping Dormouse dictated laws to a secretary snail. "Article one: anything that falls must rise again before touching the ground." Alice dropped her handkerchief: it floated upward. "That's absurd!" "Absurd?" repeated the Dormouse without opening his eyes. "No, that's rule two: absurdity is forbidden." The handkerchief became a bird and landed on Alice's nose.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Cheshire Cat appeared: first its eyes, then the void, then the grin. "Go that way," he said, pointing left, "and you'll arrive where you were yesterday. Go that way," pointing right, "and you'll be where you'll never go." Alice stood still. "Good choice," he purred. "Staying put is time travel." His grin floated for an hour after he left.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

A caterpillar smoked its hookah, blowing numbers. "Seven," it said. "No, twelve. Wait, let me recount: one, two, many." Alice coughed. "How tall are you?" "Three inches and one silly question," replied the caterpillar. "To grow, eat this mushroom. To shrink, thank it." Alice took a bite. Suddenly the world felt infinitely small and she infinitely large.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

"I'm late!" cried the White Rabbit, staring at his watch which was crying liquid minutes. Alice ran after him but the corridor bent like a straw. "Wait!" "Impossible," retorted the rabbit. "Waiting is a perishable good." He vanished into a hole no bigger than a hole. Alice squeezed in. At the bottom, she found a calendar where every day was called "Today.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Queen roared, "Off with her head!" But Alice's head had just taken a walk around her shoulders. "Your Majesty," said the head politely, "I'm not available at present. Please call back after the next dream." The playing cards fell flat. "Never seen that," muttered the Queen. Alice's head added, "Naturally not—I only came for tea." Then it reattached itself.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

Alice fell, and fell, and fell. She passed a shelf of jars labeled "yesterday," "day before yesterday," "last year." One jar was empty: "today." "You see," said a voice, "today is always empty because we fill it while falling." Alice took the jar: it weighed exactly the weight of silence. She put it on her head. The fall slowed. She had all the time to read the labels.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

"I can count to never," said the Gryphon, writing numbers on the water with his claw. "How much is never?" asked Alice. "The number after infinity—but it changes depending on who says it. For an ant, never lasts three seconds. For a stone, never never begins." The water closed. "You see," he added, "those numbers no longer exist. That's never: what was here just now.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Mock Turtle sniffled. "Sea school taught us subtraction of tides: one wave minus one wave equals one wet memory." Alice took out her slate. "And how much is two tears plus one sigh?" "A miniature ocean," replied the turtle. "But salted tears don't count on Sundays." Alice wiped her eyes. "Then I'll never cry again." "Wrong," sobbed the turtle. "Tuesdays are damp.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Knave of Hearts painted roses, singing: "Red for rages, pink for lies, white for when you've forgotten why you sing." Alice helped dip a brush. "And black?" "Black is the color of the Hatter's hat when he's drunk too much tea." Suddenly a rose turned purple. "Ah," said the Knave, "that one has understood the riddle." The riddle was: why is a rose a rose?;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

"Let's swap roles," proposed the Mouse to Alice. "You chase your tail, I'll look for my shadow." Alice ran in circles. Her tail—which she'd never had—appeared. "But I'm a girl, not a mouse!" "Then let's swap again: you be the mouse, I'll be the hole." Alice became a round hole. Through her blew the wind that tells endless stories. She greatly enjoyed being a hole.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The Time Merchant shouted: "Sale on crushed afternoons, half-price on warped dawns!" Alice bought a used second. She held it to her ear: it chimed like a cracked bell. "That's a second too many," she complained. "No," corrected the merchant, "it's a second too few. The missing ones are the most expensive." The second melted in her hand. Alice suddenly felt very hungry for never.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

Alice met a Dodo reading a newspaper whose words flew away. "Today," he read aloud, "gravity has increased by three smiles." Alice jumped: she fell slower than a snowflake. "That's pleasant!" "Yes," said the Dodo, "but tomorrow, lightness becomes mandatory. You'll have to tie yourself down with stones to avoid floating until lunch." Alice tied her shoe to an idea. The idea was heavy.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

"I shall tell you a story with no beginning and no end," said the Caterpillar, blowing smoke into a loop. "That's impossible," replied Alice. The Caterpillar smiled. "Precisely why it's a story." Alice listened. She heard her own name, then another name, then the sound of tea cooling, then nothing, then everything. "I understood nothing," she said. "Perfect," said the Caterpillar, "that's the middle of the story.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

The King of Hearts tried a biscuit accused of being eaten too early. "Witness, what did you see?" he asked Alice. "I saw the biscuit exist, then not exist." "That's a crime," declared the King. "Non-existence is forbidden after four o'clock." The biscuit—already inside the Hatter's stomach—spoke from within: "Your Majesty, I am more alive than ever." Someone cut off the word. Cut off what? No one knew.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

Alice walked to a path that split into three: a milk path, a honey path, a dried-tears path. "Which one?" she asked a pebble. "The one you won't take," replied the pebble. "True journeys are the ones you don't take." Alice sat at the crossroads. She became a path herself. Someone walked on her: it was herself, younger, running after a rabbit. "Hello," said Alice. "Hello," replied the other Alice, vanishing.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;DeepSeek

column1:
“What time is it?” Alice asked a sleeping teapot.
The teapot yawned steam. “Time is what spills when you forget to drink it.”
“That doesn’t answer my question,” Alice said.
“Oh, it does,” the teapot replied, “you simply haven’t learned to taste answers yet.”
Alice looked down: her hands were slowly turning into pocket watches, each ticking in a different direction.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

Alice asked a stone bench, “Where am I supposed to go?”
The bench creaked thoughtfully. “You are currently sitting in your destination.”
“That’s impossible,” said Alice, standing up.
“Is it?” replied the bench. “Then why do you still feel like you’ve arrived?”
Behind her, the path folded itself neatly like a letter never meant to be sent.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“What is this place?” Alice asked a floating mirror.
The mirror rippled. “A place you nearly remembered yesterday.”
“I don’t remember that,” said Alice.
“Exactly,” said the mirror, cracking slightly. “That’s why it still exists.”
Alice touched it and found her reflection already walking away, waving at someone she hadn’t met yet.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

Alice asked a clock hanging upside down, “Why are you broken?”
“I am not broken,” it said. “I am simply late in reverse.”
“That makes no sense,” Alice insisted.
“Sense is optional here,” said the clock. “Time prefers confusion—it lasts longer.”
Suddenly, all the minutes fell out of the air like raindrops and ran under the door.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“What are you reading?” Alice asked a book with no pages.
“Your hesitation,” said the book.
“I wasn’t hesitating,” Alice said quickly.
“Of course not,” it replied. “That’s why the story keeps rewriting itself.”
Alice blinked—and the world around her turned a page, forgetting to include her name.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“What is the weather today?” Alice asked a drooping sunflower.
The sunflower tilted its heavy head. “Weather is just sky remembering its moods.”
“That’s not helpful,” Alice said.
“Oh, but it is,” the flower replied. “You are currently standing inside yesterday’s rain.”
Alice looked up: the clouds were knitting themselves into a scarf around the sun, whispering apologies.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“Where does this path lead?” Alice asked a skipping stone.
The stone bounced once before answering. “It depends on how long you hesitate.”
“I’m not hesitating,” Alice said quickly.
“Then you’ll arrive somewhere else entirely,” it replied cheerfully.
At that moment, the ground beneath her feet turned into water—but politely refused to admit it.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

Alice asked a locked door, “Why won’t you open?”
The door sighed. “I am waiting for the correct version of you.”
“I am already me,” Alice said.
“Most unfortunate,” said the door. “That version never carries the right key.”
Behind her, Alice heard her own footsteps arriving late, apologizing for the delay.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“What are you writing?” Alice asked a pencil drawing in the air without paper.
“Your next mistake,” said the pencil.
“I don’t plan to make mistakes,” Alice said.
“That’s what makes it so easy,” it replied.
Suddenly, words began falling from nowhere, spelling out conversations she hadn’t had yet.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

“Who lives here?” Alice asked a house that seemed to be breathing.
“Only questions,” said the house. “Answers moved out long ago.”
“Where did they go?” Alice asked.
“They followed certainty,” the house whispered. “It is a very quiet place.”
Alice stepped closer, and the walls rearranged themselves into a memory she didn’t recognize—but missed anyway.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;ChatGPT

Alice found herself standing before a tiny door, no larger than a mouse-hole, yet it shimmered with an invitation she could not ignore. The brass handle, polished to a mirror’s gleam, seemed to wink at her as she knelt down, her skirts pooling around her like a puddle of forgotten tea.
"Open me," the door whispered, though doors do not speak, and Alice was quite certain she had never before heard one do so. But in Wonderland, certainty was as slippery as the Cheshire Cat’s grin. She turned the handle, and the door creaked open just enough for her to peer through—into a garden more vibrant than any she had ever seen. The flowers, each petal a different hue, swayed as if dancing to a tune only they could hear.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

"There’s no room!" Alice protested, for the chairs were already occupied by a dormouse, a lizard in a waistcoat, and a very cross-looking hedgehog.
"Nonsense!" the Hatter declared, tipping his hat so far back it nearly fell off. "There’s always room for those who don’t take up space!" And with that, he poured her a cup of tea that smelled faintly of lavender and regret.
Alice sipped cautiously. The liquid was warm, then cold, then warm again, as if it couldn’t decide its own temperature. "This is the queerest tea I’ve ever had," she remarked.
"Queer? My dear, you’ve not seen queer until you’ve seen the sugar cube sing!" the Hatter replied, dropping a lump of sugar into his cup. It let out a tiny, high-pitched "La-la-la!" before dissolving into silence.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

"But how ever shall I fit?" Alice mused, for she was far too tall, and the door far too small. She considered shrinking, but the thought of it made her knees wobble. Perhaps, she thought, the answer lay not in changing herself, but in changing the door. With a deep breath, she knocked politely and asked, "Might you grow a little, please?"
The door remained silent, but the handle stretched upward, and the frame expanded like a yawn. Alice, ever the polite guest, curtsied and stepped through—only to find herself not in the garden at all, but in a hallway lined with doors, each one smaller than the last.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

Alice sat beneath a tree whose branches twisted like question marks, pondering the nature of directions. "Which way ought I to go?" she asked the air, for there was no one else about.
A pair of golden eyes blinked into existence above her. "That depends," said the Cheshire Cat, his tail flicking into view like a comma.
"On what?" Alice asked, craning her neck.
"On where you want to end up," the Cat replied, his grin widening until it seemed to wrap around the tree.
"I don’t much care," Alice admitted.
"Then it doesn’t matter which way you go," the Cat said, and with that, he vanished—all but his smile, which lingered like a promise or a warning.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

Off with their heads!" the Queen screamed every time a hedgehog uncurled and wandered off.
Alice, who had never been very good at croquet, found herself cheering whenever a hedgehog escaped. "You’re not playing properly!" the Queen snapped, her face turning the color of overripe strawberries.
"I don’t think this is a proper game at all," Alice muttered, watching as her flamingo mallet sneezed and dropped its head into a bush.
"Proper?" the Queen shrieked. "Proper is for people who don’t know how to have fun!" And with that, she declared Alice the winner, which was just as well, for the hedgehogs had all gone on strike.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

The Mock Turtle sat on a rock, his shell polished to a high sheen, and sniffled into a handkerchief. "We used to dance the Lobster Quadrille," he said, "but now there’s no one left to dance with."
Alice, who had never heard of a Lobster Quadrille, sat beside him and patted his flippers. "Perhaps you could teach me?" she offered.
The Mock Turtle brightened. "Oh, could I! First, you must learn to weep like a porpoise!" He demonstrated, producing a sound like a squeaky violin. Alice tried her best, but her weeping sounded more like a teakettle.
"Not bad for a beginner," the Mock Turtle said kindly. "Now, the first step is to throw the lobsters as high as you can!"
Alice looked at the bucket of lobsters beside him. "Won’t they mind?";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

Alice, who was used to being talked about but not to being talked to by flowers, curtsied politely. "I beg your pardon," she said, "but I’ve never met a flower who could speak before."
"Oh, we can do more than speak," the violet replied, stretching its petals wide. "We can sing, we can gossip, and we can tell you the future—though we’re usually wrong about that."
A daisy, its face as bright as the sun, giggled. "She wants to know if she’ll grow up to be a queen!"
"I do not!" Alice protested, blushing.
"Well, you won’t," the rose said decisively. "You’ll be something much more interesting. You’ll be yourself.";Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

The Mad Hatter was called to the stand. "I saw the Knave," he said, "or perhaps it was a dream. Or a sandwich. It’s all rather confusing."
"Objection!" shouted the March Hare. "He’s always confusing!"
Alice, who had somehow been appointed as the court’s official note-taker, scribbled furiously. "This is the silliest trial I’ve ever seen," she whispered to the Cheshire Cat, who had appeared beside her.
"Oh, this is nothing," the Cat replied, his tail flicking. "Wait until you see the verdict."
The Queen, who had been dozing, suddenly woke up and screamed, "Off with his head!"
"But we haven’t even decided if he’s guilty!" Alice cried.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

The Mock Turtle had insisted Alice learn the Lobster Quadrille, and so she found herself on a moonlit beach, surrounded by lobsters, crabs, and a very enthusiastic turtle. "First," the Mock Turtle said, "you must throw the lobsters into the air!"
Alice picked up a lobster, who looked at her with a resigned expression. "Are you sure about this?" she asked.
"Of course!" the Mock Turtle said. "It’s tradition!"
Alice tossed the lobster gently. It soared into the air, did a somersault, and landed neatly on the head of a crab, who immediately began to dance.
"Now you’re getting it!" the Mock Turtle cheered. "Next, you must sing the song of the sea!"
Alice cleared her throat and sang the only sea song she knew, which was actually about a walrus and a carpenter. The lobsters clapped their claws, the crabs tapped their feet, and even the moon seemed to smile down at them.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI

Alice rubbed her eyes and sat up. "It was the most extraordinary dream," she said. "There was a rabbit, and a cat, and a queen who wanted to behead everyone!"
Her sister smiled. "Sounds like quite the adventure."
Alice looked down at her hands, half-expecting them to still be the wrong size. "It was," she said softly. "And I think… I think I’ll miss it."
Her sister closed her book. "Well," she said, "if it’s any consolation, I don’t think Wonderland is the sort of place that lets go of you easily. I suspect you’ll be back."
Alice smiled. "I hope so," she said.
And with that, she leaned back against the tree, watching the stars begin to twinkle, and wondered what other adventures might be waiting for her—just beyond the edge of sleep.;Alice's Adventures in Wonderland;MistralAI
